headline: GRAINS DRIFT LOWER AS PLANTING PICKS UP
subheadline: Small declines across corn and soybeans while oats rally 1.7% on quality concerns.
lead: Corn futures eased fractionally to $4.58 nearby while soybeans slipped to $11.59 as early planting progress removes some weather premium. The action remains muted as farmers balance between field work and pricing decisions, though oats bucked the trend with a solid 1.7% gain to $3.48 on persistent quality concerns from last year's challenging harvest.
teaser: Quiet drift lower in grains as planting season builds momentum — but weather still holds the cards.

one_number.context: Down just 0.2% to $4.76, December corn is holding remarkably steady despite early planting progress removing weather premiums. This stability suggests the market remains wary of potential planting delays ahead — and for good reason, with optimal planting windows narrowing each day into May.

sections.title: Grains Ease Gently
sections.body: Corn and soybeans drifted lower in subdued trade as early planting progress removes some weather urgency. Nearby corn at $4.58 and soybeans at $11.59 reflect a market still cautious but no longer panicked about supply. December corn's modest 0.2% decline to $4.76 shows new crop pricing holding steady despite reduced planting fears. The real story is what didn't happen — no dramatic selloff despite good planting weather across much of the Corn Belt.
sections.bottom_line: Market breathing easier but staying alert as planting season unfolds.
sections.farmer_action: Use any weather scares to price remaining old crop aggressively — these steady levels won't last if we hit extended wet weather.

sections.title: Cattle Retreat
sections.body: Live cattle fell 1.4% to $247.60 while feeders dropped 1.0% to $368.35, pulling back from recent highs as profit-taking emerged. Class III milk at $17.03 continued yesterday's weakness theme, though the 0.2% decline was mild compared to recent volatility. Lean hogs held steady at $101.70, showing resilience in a down livestock session. These pullbacks feel like normal consolidation after strong runs rather than fundamental shifts.
sections.bottom_line: Livestock taking a breather after recent strength — nothing alarming here.
sections.farmer_action: Cattle producers should view any significant weakness as protection-buying opportunities — margins remain historically strong.

sections.title: Energy Mixed Story
sections.body: WTI crude fell 2.3% to $87.74 as supply concerns eased, but natural gas rallied 1.4% to $2.69 on seasonal demand expectations. This creates a mixed bag for input costs — diesel prices may ease while propane-powered grain dryers face higher costs. The energy complex continues balancing geopolitical risk premiums against demand fundamentals, keeping volatility elevated across the sector.
sections.bottom_line: Energy costs pulling in different directions — diesel relief, gas heating up.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining diesel needs while prices soften — planting season demand typically reverses crude weakness quickly.

sections.title: Steady Macro Backdrop
sections.body: The S&P 500 gained 0.3% to $7,041 while the dollar held flat at 100.00, providing a stable foundation for commodity markets. Gold at $4,823 and silver's 1.0% gain to $79.78 suggest inflation hedging demand remains active despite broader market calm. This macro stability removes one variable from agricultural pricing, allowing weather and supply fundamentals to drive the conversation.
sections.bottom_line: Financial markets cooperating — lets ag fundamentals take center stage.
sections.farmer_action: Focus on operational decisions rather than macro hedging — currency and financial stability reduce complexity.

the_more_you_know.title: Oats: The Forgotten Grain's Quality Story
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 1.7% oats rally to $3.48 highlights a persistent quality problem from 2025's challenging harvest. While corn and soybeans grab headlines, oats processors are paying premiums for clean, plump grain suitable for human consumption. Much of last year's crop came in weather-damaged, leaving feed-quality supplies adequate but food-grade oats scarce. For producers considering oats in rotation, quality premiums are creating profit opportunities that dwarf the per-acre returns on major crops.

USDA crop progress report — corn planting pace nationally
Any rain threats across prime planting regions

daily_quote.text: Farming: where every year you bet the farm on the weather, the market, and the government — and still show up next spring.

source_summary: CBOT futures, CME livestock contracts, NYMEX energy
